14. Legal fictions.
The common law allowed certainfictionsin pleading; as, in the action of ejectment,
thestatementof a demise tothe nominal plaintiff; and , in troveror detinue,the allegation
thatthedefendant found the goods , which was seldom true; and , when itwas not neces-
saryto prove the number , quantity, species, or value of a thing preciselyas laid, itwas
usualto statea greaternumber,& c. 1 Chitty'sPl.,230. But itseemsclearthatthepro-
visionsof the Code requiringa statementof factsconstitutingthe plaintiff'scause of action,
andthe verificationof pleadings,have abolished allthose fictionsexcepting " allegations
concerningvalueor damage,not accompaniedby an allegationof an expresspromise, or
bya statementof factsshowing an implied promise,to pay such value or damage "-which
allegationsneed not be verified. Code, 116. And the common law also requiredcertain
fictions: thus, in actionseven upon transitorycausesof action, such as tortsto the person
orpersonalproperty,the plaintiffwas requiredto allegethatthe tortwas committedin
someparticularcounty in England ,though itwas committed in some othercounty, or even
inaforeigncountry. 1 Ch.Pl.,271. ItisclearthattheCode requiresa truestatementof
thevenueinlocalactions,and does not requireany statementof itin transitoryactions.
As towhat are localand what are transitoryactions, see Code, titleV.
15.As to " givingcolor."
The common law frequentlyrequireda defendant,pleadingan affirmativedefence, to
admitexpresslythe matterchargedby the plaintiff, which was called" givingcolor" (I
Ch.Pl., 502); and itwas saidin Harper v .Harper , 10 Bush , 454, (decided in 1874) that a
pleaofjustificationin an actionfor slander" must , in general, confessthe publicationas
laidinthedeclaration; and thisin consequence of a rulewhich requiresthe party pleading
eitherto confess the previous matter and avoid it, or to traverseit." But the present
Code declaresthat " an expressadmission of a factisnot necessaryto rendervalida plead-
inginavoidance or estoppelthereof." ? 113, subs. 6.
16. As toforming an issue.
The common law requiredcertainformal conclusionsof pleadings in order to form an
issue. Thus, ifa plea, replication, or rejoinderof a party, containedaffirmativealle-
gations,he was bound to aver " and thishe is ready to verify;" whilst, if it contained a
traverse,he was bound to say" and thishe praysmay be inquiredof by thecountry," or
" and of thishe puts himself upon the country;" and, when a party had thus " put him-
selfupon the country," the otherpartywas bound to fileanotherpleasayingthat" he
doth likewise," which was called the similiter.
But thisCode ,though itrequirespartiesto plead to an issue, dispenseswith those for-
malitiesby declaring that " there shallbe no reply, nor additionalpleading, except to
affirmativeallegationsof an adverse pleading." 112.

CHAP .VII.] 167GENERAL RULES OF PLEADING .
Inactionsfor libelor slanderthe only defencesare such as deny the publishingof the
slanderor suchas justifyit. 10 Bush ,454 .
1. AS TO DENIALS .
The Code containsno specialprovisionon thissubject. Accordingto the common
law,thedefendantcould not, under a plea of " not guilty," prove the truth of the alleged
slander,even to reduce damages (I Ch .Pl., 488 ; Litt. S. C., 159); and that rule has not
beenchangedby the Code as to an answer of denial. Harper v .Harper , 10 Bush ,447 .
AccordingtoChitty,a defendantcould, undera plea of " notguilty," prove justifica-
tion; as,thatthe words were spoken by the defendant as counsel, and were pertinent to
theissue(1Ch.Pl.,489); but itwas held,in Kentucky,thatfactsconstitutinga justifi-
cationmust be speciallypleaded. 5. J. M. , 186 ; 3 Dana , 433 ; 5 ld., 323 : and that
rulehasnot been changed by the Code . 10 Bush , 456 .
2. PLEAS OF JUSTIFICATION .
( 1) Pleas averring truth of the alleged slander.
a. Itwas formerlyheld that such plea must expresslyadmit the speaking of the words .
Litt.S. C., 158; Harper v.Harper , 10 Bush , 459- decided in 1874. But see ? 113, subs.
6 of thisCode , which declaresthat " an express admission of a fact isnot necessaryto
rendervalida pleading in avoidance thereof."
b. Such plea isbad on demurrer unlessitjustifyall the actionablewords charged by
theplaintiff: thus,inan actionby Bond forsaying" Bond isa thief; he hasstolencorn,"
a pleathatthe plaintiffisa thiefisinsufficient. I Litt., 35 ; Litt. S. C.,158.
c. Under the old practice, forthe purpose of reducing damages , the defendant could,
withoutpleadingit,proveany mitigatingfactwhich did not amount toa justification: as,
thatthe words were only a statement of what another had said( Williamsv . Greenwade,
3 Dana ,432 ,or expresseda suspicionresultingfrom rumored facts(I В. М. , 171) ; or that
theplaintiffprovokedthedefendantto use thewords by " injuriousacts,or disparaging
epithets" (5 Dana , 323); or had , on a previous occasion, assailedthe character of the
defendant( Morehead v.Jones, 2 B. M. , 210 ; 15 Id., 198); or- though the plaintiff'salle-
gationof good characterismere matter of inducement and not traversable-" the general
characterof the plaintiffand his standing in society," but not his general character in
relationto the factsput in issue. 2 Bibb, 24 .
Section124 is a mere declarationof the rulejust stated except so faras said section
permitsa defendant to statemitigatingcircumstances in a plea averringthe truth of the
allegedslander- a statement which , as it does not affectthe merits of the controversy,
butonlythequestionof damages,was redundantundertheold practice,and would have
been so under the Code but for the permission given in 124. Can it be reasonably
contendedthat the legislatureintended, by that section, to requirethe defendant to allege
theplaintiff'sbad character,in ordertoaffectthe questionof damages? And itseemsto
me that the following dictum in Harper v . Harper, 10 Bush , 458- viz.,that " this provis-
ionabrogatesthe rule that an unsustained plea of justificationisevidence of malice and
will[may ] enhance the damages "-is erroneous. Indeed ,I do not perceivehow the legis-
laturecould,ifitwould,controla juryupon sucha question. Itisclear,atany rate,that
the legislaturedid not attempt to do so as to otherjustificationsthan pleasof truth.
(2) Pleas that the words were used justifiably.
" No words , writtenor spoken ,willbe actionable,unlessthey had been published with
malice, expressor implied. And though the law will, prima facie,generallyimply malice
fromthefalsehoodof a slanderousimputation; yetthemanner and occasionof the publi-
cationmay rebut such an implicationand impose on the plaintiffthe burthen of proving
express malice ." 1 B. M. , 168 .
Thus,maliceisnot presumed from a confidentialcommunicationby one who isinter-
ested, or to one who isinterested; as, a statementconcerning the characteror conduct of
a servant, made to hisemployer or toone who contemplatesemployinghim; ora state

ment by a person, or to a person,who has been robbed , with a view to the detectionof
the criminal(Faris v.Starke,9 Dana , 128; Grimes v .Coyle, 6 B. M. , 301 ; Hart v.Recd,
1 Id., 166 ; 1 Ch .Pl., 489); or from expression of suspicion, with proper precautions,to
the family of a person whose house has been set on fire, as to who is the incendiary(79
Ky ., 207); or from a statementmade by a member of a church or of a masonic lodge,ina
church-controversy or masonic -controversy- aliteras to statements by one who is not a
member (Lucas v. Case, 9 Bush , 297 ; Nix v .Caldwell, 81 Ky .,293); or from a statement
toa justice,upon an applicationfora warrantforfelony(4 Bibb, 38); or from a libelous
statementin a billin chancery. II B. M. , 51.
In such cases,the occasion, & c., raiseonly a prima faciepresumption of the defend-
ant'sinnocence. But , as was said in Faris v .Starke, 9 Dana , 131, " in some cases, where
the publicationoccurs in the performance of a legalduty , the occasion is an absolute
bar to an action;" as,in the case of an attorneyor a witness in a law -suit, withregard
to statementswhich are pertinenttothematterin question. Morgan v . Booth, 13 Bush ,480 ;
Stewartv .Hall, 83 Ky ., 375. And in Morgan v .Booth, that rulewas applied in favor ofa
defendant who spoke the slanderouswords whilstconducting his own defence before a
justiceof the peace.
And in Hardin v .Cumstock,2 Mar. , 480, the court said " we are aware of no case
where slanderousmatter charged [by the plaintiff] in a regularcourse of justicehas ever
been held to be libelous;" and thatthe remedy of a defendant, libelouslysued,was an
actionformaliciousprosecution.

the defendant had said itwas generally reported" that Mrs. Anderson was guilty of

Thissortof curehas been frequentlyspoken of as being " intendment afterverdict;"
butitseems clearthat, as to thismatter, the decisionof a chancellor, upon the evidence,
isentitledto the same effectas the verdict of a jury : see Deatly'sheirsv . Murphy , 3
Mar., 472 ; Neilson, & c.,v . Churchill,& c., 5 Dana , 333 ; Wall and Burnsides v .Hill, 7
Dana ,172; Garton'sheirsv .Bates, 4 B. M. , 366 ; Hackwith v .Damrou , I Mon. , 235.
But therehas been some conflictof opinion concerning the curative effectof a verdict
ordecision,upon a trialof facts,asisshown by definitionsaswellas decisionsoftheCourt
ofAppeals .
In Tyev.Catching,78 Ky .,463,whichwas an actioninequity, itappearedthatCatch-
ingerecteda dam and millon a stream,aboutone mile above Tye'sdam and mill; and
that, shortlythereafter,Tye repairedhisdam , and,as charged by Catching, unlawfully
raisedithigher than itever was before, and thereby caused the water to flow back on the
wheel of Catching'smill,doing him greatdamage ; and the prayer of the petitionwas for
damages,and that Tye be compelled to lower the dam to itsformer height; Tye denied
theallegationsof the petition; and,upon hearing,the courtadjudgeddamages against
him,and decreedthatthe dam be lowered twelve inches, and the Court of Appeals
said :
" The rulein equityis,thatthestatementof the causeof actionshouldbe setforth
withsuch particularityas to enablethe chancellor, from an inspectionof the petition
alone, to grant the reliefsought. If the petitionisnot so specific,therecan be no decree,
by default, on failureof the defendant to controvertthe matter setup ; nor can there be a
decree, afterpleading over by the defendant, unless the answer isof such a nature as to
curethe defectin the petition, and , when taken in connection with the petition, to show
thereliefsoughtand the plaintiff'srightthereto. In such a case,in orderthat the
answer may possesssuch curativeproperties, itmust supply the missing averments in the
petition,without which itwould be fatallydefective. In the case under consider-
ation, the petitionfailsto allegethe height of the dam at any time priorto the building
of theappellee'sdam and mill,and alsofailsto statehow much appellantTye added to
theheightof thedam . The lastmentionedallegationwas undoubtedlyessentialto pre-
sentan issue, and to enable the court to decree in conformity to the prayer of the petition.
No decreecouldhave gone ifno answerhad been filed; and asthe answer simplydenies
theallegationsof the petition, and does not expressly, or by implication, supply the
omittedallegationsof thepetition,thedefectisnot cured."
Concerning thatcaseI suggest:
(1) That , as shown above , the decision of a chancellor, upon the evidence in a
cause, has thesame curativeeffectas the verdictof a jury with regardto defective
pleadings .
(2) That theopinionof thecourt,inholdingthatthe answer in connectionwith the
petitionmust show thereliefsoughtand theplaintiff'srightthereto,withoutregardtothe
evidence,isopposedto a previouslywellsettleddoctrine: seeBarlowv.Wiley,3 Mar.,457,
andDeatlyv.Murphy,Id.,474, above cited; and Hackwithv.Damrou ,& c., I Mon.,235,
inwhich reliefwas givenupon a defectivebillin chancery,becauseitwas " aidedsome-
whatby theanswersand more by theexhibitsin the cause;" and Garton'sheirsv.Bates,
4 B. M. ,366, which was a suitfordower , and the billfailedto allegethat the husband

180 [TITLE VII .PLEADINGS .
had the legaltitle; but, the defendant having denied that fact, itwas held that, " upon
the pleadingsand proof,the complainant has a rightto be endowed :" see, also Louisville
& PortlandCanal Co. v .Murphy , 9 Bush , 522, and Smith v .Theobald, 86 Ky ., 141.
(3) The opinion is partlybased on the alleged ground that, if there had been no
answer there could have been no judgment by default: why not ? I concede that alle-
gationsof legalconclusionsdo notauthorizea judgment by default. But thegrievance
complained of by Catching was the backing of water upon his wheel , and itwas sufficient
forhim to allegethatTye did so by raisinghis dam , the factthatTye had raisedit twelve
inches, or to any particularheight,beingmatterof evidence; just as it is sufficientfor the
plaintiff, in an action upon a warranty of the soundness of a horse, to allegethat the
horsewas unsound,thefactthatthehorsehad glanders,or any otherparticulardisease,
being matter of evidence. 3 Mon. , 173.
True , the petitiondid not enable the court to decide how much the dam should be
lowered,or what damages shouldbe awarded. But thatwas immaterial. The petition
showed thatthe plaintiffwas entitledto some damages (13 B. M. , 405 ; 2 Duv ., 419 ) ; and
to have Tye's dam lowered to some extent; and , iftherehad been no answer, there should
have been a judgment by default; and an inquiryunder 379 of the Code would have
enabled the court to decide as to the extent of relief.
(4) But ,even if itwere true that Catching would not have been entitledto a judgment
by defaultifthere had been no answer, that factwould not prove the correctnessof the
decision; because Tye answered denying plaintiff'sallegations, and it must be assumed
that the evidencejustifiedthe decision of the chancellor. See 6 B. M. , 381, and other
casescitedin note (2), ante, page 178, and in note b,post, page 181.
In Drake'sadm'r v.Semonin & Dixon ,82 Ky ., 291,thecourt said: " Where there isany
defect, imperfection, or omission, in any pleading, whether in substance or form , which
would have been fatalon demurrer ; yet, ifthe issuejoined be such as necessarilyrequired,
on the trial,proofof the factsso defectivelystatedor omitted,and withoutwhich it is
not tobe presumed thateitherthejudgewould directthejurytogive,or thejurywould
have given the verdict, such defect, imperfection, or omissioniscuredby the verdict.
Chitty'sPleadings; Blisson Code Pleading; Leman's Pleading and Practice. It is often
difficultto applythisrule,and to make the properdistinctionbetween caseswhere the
cause of actionisdefectivelystated, and where there isa totalomissionto statea
cause of action, or the absence of an averment constitutingthe foundation of the plain-
tiff'sright of recovery. In Wilson v .Hunt's adm'r, 6 B. Monroe , it is said: 'When the
verdictcan be fairlyconsidered as establishingbetween the partiesthe very factwhich
shouldhave been,but isnot, preciselyaverredinthe declaration,and especiallywhen it
clearlyappearsthattheparticularfactwas understoodby thepartiestobe thepointinissue
tobe decidedby thejury,itwould be unnecessaryfortheendsofjustice,and would be more
than uselessto remand the case,that it should again be presentedforthe considerationof
thejury.' The betterrule,and in factthe one recognizedby alltheelementarywriters,
includingMr. Chitty,is,thatwhere thereisa totalomissiontostatea causeof action, or
some factessentialto the cause of action has been whollyomitted, the verdict will not
cure the defect ."
It seems clearthat the court erred in saying that where " some factessentialto the
cause of actionhas been whollyomitted,the verdictwillnot cure the defect," and in saying
that Mr. Chitty " recognized" that rule. To the contrary, the rule as to " intendment
afterverdict" was established, partly, forthe purposeof givingreliefto a plaintiffwho
has " wholly omitted" the statement of " some fact essentialto the cause of action," and
itisso statedby Chitty, even in the passage citedby the court; for he there saysthat any
omission,whether in substanceor form ,may be cured by verdict, ifthe issuejoined be such
as to requireproof of the factso omitted; and elsewhere, Chittysays: " The omission of
the averment of the performance of a condition precedent, or of an excuse for non -per-

omissionmay , in some cases,be aidedby the common law intendment,thateverything
may be presumed to have been proved which was necessaryto sustainthe action, for a
verdictwill cure a case defectivelystated." Vol. 1,page 319.
The rulestatedby Chittyseemstobe supportedby thedecidedweightof Kentucky
authority; though,asI have suggested,therehas been some conflictofopinionupon this
subject, which leadsme to refersomewhat particularlyto the cases pro and con.

§ 138 [165]. Verificationunnecessaryaftertrialbegins.-No objection
shallbe taken, afterthe commencement of the trial, to any pleading
forthe want of, or a defectin, the verification.
1.Objectionto a pleading,forwant of verification,shouldbe made by motion. 15
Β .Μ .. 628 ; 3 Bush , 225 .
2. The plaintiffhaving moved ,afterthe jury was sworn , to strikefrom the recordthe
defendant's amended answer , because it had not been verified; itwas held that the defend-
ant should have been allowed to verifyit. 3 Bush , 225.
3. In Butler, & c., v .Church oftheImmaculate Conception, 14 Bush , 540, thedefendant,
having been permittedtowithdraw itsanswer,moved thatthe plaintiffbe requiredto
verifyhis petition," and his failureto do so was punished ly a dismissalof his suit;"
and the Court of Appeals said: " This rulingof the courtwas erroneous. This courthas
decided substantiallythat, afteranswer filedby the defendant, it istoo lateto insistthat
the plaintiffshallswear to hispleading, because, by the failureto make the questiontill
afteranswer,the defendant has waived his right to make it. The plea,that
the plaintiffhas not sworn to his petition,isin the nature of the plea in abatement,which,
at the common law,could never be insistedon unlessaptlyreliedon before an answerwas
put intothe meritsof the controversy."
I have not been able to findany previousdecisionto thateffectunder thepresent
Code ; and the common law rule, forbiddinga plea in abatement aftera plea tothe merits,
has no bearing on the question. Section 165 of the Code of 1854 declared that" No
objection shallbe taken, afterjudgment , to any pleading, for the want of, or defectin,
the verification;" and itwas held that defendant's motion, aftera trialand verdictfor
the plaintiff, that he be required to verifyhis petition, came too late; not becausethe
defendant had answered the petition,but because his conduct " has been such as to induce
hisadversarytobelieve,and toacton thebelief,thattheobjectionhad been abandoned."
Meador v . Turpin, 4 Met .,93. In conformityto the decision in that case, 138 of the
presentCode declaresthat " no objectionshallbe taken, afterthe commencement of
thetrial,to any pleadingforthewant of, or a defectin,theverification" [of pleading].
As " a trialis a judicialexaminationof the issuesoflaw or factin an actionorpro
ceeding" (Code, 2311); and as a demurrer or answer is necessaryto form an issueof law
or fact, itseemsclearthatthelegislaturedid not intendthatthe filingof such pleading
shouldbe a waiverof the defendant'srightto requirea verificationof the petition,by
motion made at any time before commencement of the trial.

or defence be thereby introduced.

The making of such order before the filing of such affidavit is an error of the court and not a clerical misprision. 14 B. M., 271.

§ 231 [252]. *Surplus to be returned to defendant.*—Any surplus of the attached property, or of its proceeds, shall be returned to the defendant.

§ 232 [254]. *Enforcement of bond for attached property.*—The court may, by summary proceedings, compel the delivery to the sheriff, for sale, of any attached property, for which a bond may have been given; or, for such sums of money as may be due upon such bonds, execution may issue as upon replevin-bonds.

§ 233 [255]. *Powers of court as to property, sales, &c.*—The court may, from time to time, make and enforce proper orders respecting the property, sales, and the confirmation thereof; and the application and payment of the moneys collected.

§ 234 [256]. *Retaking of property by sheriff.*—It may order the sheriff to re-possess himself, for the purpose of selling it, of any attached property which may have passed out of his hands without having been sold or converted into money; and the sheriff shall, under such order, have the same power to take the property as upon an order of attachment.

§ 235 [257]. *Reference of attachments to commissioner.*—If several attachments be executed on the same property, the court, on the motion of any one of the attaching plaintiffs, may order a reference to a commissioner, to ascertain and report the amounts and priorities of the several attachments.

§ 236 [258]. *Motion against plaintiff for additional security.*—The defendant may, at any time before judgment, after reasonable notice to the plaintiff, move the court for additional security on the part of the plaintiff; and if, on such motion, the court be satisfied that the surety in the plaintiff's bond have died or removed from this State, or is not sufficient for the amount thereof, it may vacate the order of attachment and direct restitution of any property taken under it, unless in a reasonable time, to be fixed by the court, sufficient security be given by the plaintiff.

It was held that the defendant may appeal from a judgment sustaining an attachment, though he may have assigned the attached property, because, if the Court of Appeals should hold that the attachment was wrongfully obtained, the defendant would have a right to maintain an action against the plaintiff for malicious prosecution. *Francis v. Burnett*, 84 Ky., 26, 34, in which the court and counsel overlooked *Spring & Stepp v. Besore*, 12 B. M., 555, and *Kaye v. Kean*, 18 Id., 846, according to which the judgment sustaining the attachment, though reversed, was conclusive evidence of probable cause, and defeated the defendant's right to sue for malicious prosecution.

§ 268 [294]. *Discharge of attachment for irregularity—amendments.*

1. The defendant, on reasonable notice to the plaintiff, may, at any time before the term next after the levy of the attachment, move the judge of the court, in vacation, to discharge the attachment, on the ground of its having been issued contrary to the provisions of article one of this chapter; and the judge, on an inspection of the papers in the action, or of authenticated copies of them, shall have the same power of discharging the attachment, by his written order, filed by the clerk, as he would have in court; and shall have like power to permit amendments by the plaintiff.

2. A plaintiff may, by an amended affidavit, conforming to § 196, cure a defect in the affidavit upon which he obtained an attachment; or may state a ground of attachment not mentioned in his first affidavit, whether it may have existed when he filed the first affidavit, or may have arisen afterward; and may thus acquire a lien upon the attached property, if the proceedings conform to law in other respects; but such lien shall not affect a *bona fide* right to, or lien previously acquired upon, the property, by attachment or otherwise.

The Code of 1854 contained only the first subdivision of this section.

An act of March 15, 1862, declared that "in any proceeding by attachment, now pending or hereafter commenced, the affidavit or ground of attachment may be amended, so as to embrace any grounds of attachment that may exist up to and until the final judgment upon the same. If the amendments embrace only grounds existing at the commencement of the proceeding, the lien created by suing out or levying the original attachment shall be held good; but, if the amendments embrace new grounds, not existing at the time of suing out the original attachment, the lien shall exist on the property levied on from the filing of the same." *Sess. Acts*, 92.

1. In cases under the Code of 1854, relating to defective statements of grounds of attachment existing when it was obtained, or to defective statements as to the nature, justice, or amount of the plaintiff's demand, it was held that the plaintiff might amend a *defective petition* on which he had obtained an attachment under § 476 (*Lewis v. Quinker*, 2 Met., 284); or under § 221 (*Clarke v. Seaton*, 18 B. M., 230); or might amend a *defective affidavit* on which an attachment was obtained under § 221. *Allen v. Brown*, 4 Met., 342 (overruling *Poole v. Webster*, 3 Met., 282); *Bailey v. Beadles & Bolinger*, 7 Bush, 383.

2. It was held that the amendment of March 15, 1862, relates only to the grounds of the attachment, and authorizes amendments merely for the purpose of stating grounds

II. ENJOINING PROSECUTION OF ACTIONS OR PROCEEDINGS AT LAW .
It seems to have been the practice,in some of the States,fora defendant in an action
at law to filea billforan injunctionagainstprosecutionof it,upon the ground that he had

junctions, 2259,60 .
In Kentucky ,before the adoption of the Code , the practiceseems to have been for a

defaultand then sue foran injunction. See note I, ante,page 227.
Though the Code does not expresslyprohibitan injunctionagainstprosecution of an

equitabledefencethenknown by him toexist.

asthepreventionof vexatiousorpalpablyunjustlitigation. Thus,where thelaw allowed

According to a dictum in Hillman v .Hurley, 82 Ky ., 629, " the same characterof pos-

For saidactof 1854and comments thereon,seenoteb,ante,page 202. As saidenact-

" An Actregulatingactionsforinjurytorealestatein thisCommonwealth.- Thatthe

The only differencebetween those actsconsistsin the fact that the lattercontainsthe

The court, in Hillman v .Hurley, citesno decision in support of itsdictum; and I feel

4. EFFECT OF VERDICT .
(1) Under the old practice.
Itwas settledthat the court may decide an issueaccording to the evidence, notwith-
standinga verdictto the contrary (Owens v .Owens , Hardin , 154 ; Goodloev .McLanathan ,
6Mon.,310; Moorev.Payne, 7 Dana ,380; Leev.Beatty,8 Id.,207); though, indoubtful
cases, the verdictshould be entitledto some weight. 8 Dana , 207, 212.
In Owens v . Owens , Hardin , 154, the chancellor set aside a verdict in favor of the
plaintiffwithoutstating, of record,any reasonfordoing so,and submittedthe issueto
anotherjurywhich found for the defendant,and thereuponthe chancellordismissedthe
plaintiff'sbill. The Court of Appeals held thatthe settingasideof the firstverdict
withoutshowing , of record,the reason for doing so,was erroneous; and , that courtbeing
of the opinion that the firstverdictwas supported by the evidence, the decree was re-
versedand the cause remanded with directionsto entera decree forthe complainant on
thefirstverdict" unlessthe defendant shall show some sufficientcause, to be stated on
therecordby the court, forsettingitaside." But it seems to have been held in Goodloe
v.McLanathan, 6 Mon., 310,and Moorev.Payne,7 Dana ,380,thatthe chancellormay ,
withoutenteringhisreasonsthereforupon the record,disregarda verdictor severalver-
dictsand decide the cause as to him may seem equitable.
InRuckerv.Howard,2 Bibb,169,itseemstohavebeen heldthattheCourtofAppeals
must regard the verdictas conclusiveunlessitbe setasideby the chancellor. That isun-
questionablytrue, ifthe record failsto statethe evidence. Reading v .Ford , I Bibb, 340.
But iftherecordstatestheevidenceitseemsthattheCourtof Appealsmay disregardthe
verdictthough the chancellormay have approved it. Goodloev.McLanathan,6 Mon.,
310; Moore v .Payne, 7 Dana , 380.
(2) Under the Code .
In Moore'sheirsv.Shepherd, 2 Duv ., 131, the Court of Appeals, in an opinion
deliveredby Judge Robertson,held thatour Code of Practicehad so changed thecom-
mon lawas to give to a verdictof a jury,upon an issuein chancery,as much effectas
a verdictin a common -law action would be entitled to on a motion for a new trial. But
inHendrix,& c.,v.Money,& c., I Bush,306,thatcourt,inan opinionwhich was alsode-
liveredby Judge Robertson,withoutreferringto Moore'sheirsv.Shepherdortothe Code
ofPractice,said: " The juryhavingbeen empanneled onlytohelpthe chancellortothe
rightconclusion,theirfindingisnot entitledtothe more conclusiveeffectof an ordinary
verdict ."
If,however,an equitableissuebe submittedtoa juryattheinstanceoftheparties,the
verdictwillbe entitledto as much effectasa verdictin an ordinayaction. Moore'sheirs
v.Shepherd, 2 Duv ., 132.

II . GENERAL RULES AS TO INSTRUCTIONS .
1. Duty ofcourtas togiving instructions.
It isnot the duty of the court to instructthe jury as to points of law unlessa motion
be made forthatpurpose(Hardin, 535),though the courtmay do so (7 J.J. M. , 197);
and ifan improperinstructionbe askedfor,thecourtmay overruleitwithoutgivingany
instructionon thepoint. 1 Bibb, 159; 7J.J.M. , 197. But,though not bound todo so,
thecourtmay modifyan instructiontomeet thelaw of the case(4 Dana ,424; 7 Id.,403;
7 Β .Μ ., 569; 8 Id.,42; 11 Bush, 507); ormay refusetogiveinstructionsaskedforand
give othersintheirstead. 5 B. M. , 42 ; 12 Id., 249.
And itistheduty of thecourt,on motionof a party,toinstructthejury,not onlyon
points of law presentedby the issues, but as to what would be the effectof a verdict, if
therebe a disputeon thequestion. Delanyv.Vaughn,3 Bibb,379.
2. Duty of court as togiving written instructions.
The failureof the court to reduce itsinstructionsto writing, when required to do so
by a party, isground for reversal. But , the courthaving given oralinstructions, toldthe
jury thattheycouldretire,and thatthe instructionswould be reducedtowriting, and
thatiftheyhad any difficulty,theycouldsendforthem ; and the plaintiff'scounselsaid
he hoped that the court would do so : held, that thiswas not a requirement of written
instructions. 1 Met .,83 .
3. Law tobe decidedby court- factsbyjury.
The courtshoulddecideallquestionsof law,by instructions,and submit onlycontro-
vertedfactsto the jury. 2 Met ., 553.
Consequently, thecourthastherighttoinstructthejurythattheymust regardastrue,
forthe purposes of the action, a designated factwhich is not controverted by the plead.
ings(3 Met.,56); and itiserroneousto instructthejurythattheymust findthata note
sued on was executed by the defendant, when the executionof itwas not put in issue(18
B. M. , 226); or that they may find, from the evidence, the non -existenceof a fact the
existenceof which isadmitted by the pleadings. 5 Bush ,474 .
And an instructionwhich submits a question of law to the jury is erroneous; as,an
instructionthat " the plaintiffmust eitherprove an express contract or one necessarily
implied from the circumstances," without sayingfrom what circumstancesa contractwould
necessarilybe implied(I B. M. , 122); or thateverymaterialallegationof a pleading,not
controvertedby an adverse pleading, must be taken as true, without showing what alle-
gationswere not controverted. 1 Met ., 570 ; 2 Id., 301 ; 7 Bush , 198.
4. Abstract instructions.
" Every propositionisdeemed abstract,however pertinentand applicableitmay be,
unlessitbe made toapplyin expresstermseitherto the attitudeof thepartiesor tothe
very factsinissue." 7J.J. M. , 197.
And itiserroneous to give an instructionwhich , though correct as a matter of law,is
hypothecatedupon facts,or upon thenon-existenceof facts,which theevidencedoesnot
conduce to prove (I Mon., 126; 3 Id.,534; 1 Dana ,38; 11 B. M.,41; 13Id.,486); or
which relatesto the weightof conflictingtestimony(I B. M. ,291; 17 Id.,28 ; 8 Bush,

(a) GENERAL VERDICTS .
1. Certainty in verdict.
" Though theverdictmay not concludeformallyor punctuallyin the words of the
issue,yet, if the point in issuecan be concluded out of the finding, the court shallwork
theverdictintoform and make itserve. Verdicts are not to be taken strictlylike plead-
ings,butthecourtwillcollectthe meaning of thejury,iftheygive such a verdictthat
thecourtcan understandthem." 1 Bibb,251,257; 2 Id., 178,429; 10 B. М. ,55.
Thus,thepetitionallegedthatthedefendantbroke the plaintiff'sclose,and assaulted
andbeattheplaintiff. The defendantdeniedthe allegations. The evidenceproved the
assaultupon the plaintiff, but did not conduce to prove the breaking of the close: held,
thata verdictfindingthe defendantguilty" as chargedin the petition" was sufficient.
13 В. М. ,204.
And in an action on a note or bond for money , a verdict finding for the plaintiffa
certainsum and interestthereon from a given date, or finding" for the plaintiffthe debt
inthepetitionmentioned," or finding" forthe plaintiff," authorizesjudgment for the
debtandinterestuntilthe finding. 6.J.J. M. , 549; 12 В. М. ,506; 1 Duv ., 94.
And itwas held that, in an action of debt , a verdictfor a sum in damages was not an
availableerror. Hardin, 182; Litt. S.C., 122; 9 Dana ,345; 9 B. M. ,440.
And that,upon an issueof guiltyor notguilty,inan actionforassaultand battery,a
verdictwhich,without findingthe defendantsguilty,found damages againstthem in a
certainsum,authorizeda judgment thereforinfavorof theplaintiff, althoughtheverdict
" didnotsay,insomany words,thatthedamages were foundfortheplaintiff." 7J.J. M. ,
341 .
And that,ifthejurybe sworn totrytwo ormore issues,a verdictassessingdamages in
favorof theplaintiffmust be regardedas a findingagainstthe defendantupon allthe
issues. Hardin,291; 1 Bibb,250; 4 Id.,497.
And that,ifthejurybe sworn to try " the issue," when thereare severalissues,a
verdictassessingdamages infavorof theplaintiffwillbe regardedasresponsivetoallthe
issues. 1Bibb,337; 3 J.J. M. , 682.
And ,thequestionbeingwhetherhogs belongedto A or B when an attachmentwas
levied,averdictfindingthem tobe thepropertyofA ,withoutfindingthattheywerehis
whentheattachmentwas levied,was heldtobe sufficient. 2 Met .,209.

But where , in an actionagainstan executrix, one count alleged a promise by the tes-
tator, and another count alleged a promise by the executrix, itwas held that a general
verdictforthe plaintiffdid not entitlehim to a judgment againstthe defendant, eitheras
executrixor individually. 6 J. J. M. , 458.
2. Verdicts upon immaterial issues.
Judgment should not be rendered upon a verdicton immaterial issues(5 Mon. , 273 ;
10 B. M. , 156), though therebe a materialissue(2 Bibb, 226); or upon a general verdict
on two issues, one of which is immaterial. 5 Litt., 176 ; 1 Mon. , 247 ; 5 Id., 11, 34 ; 4
J. J. M. , 554 ; 7 Dana , 171. But see 2 Bibb, 226, and Burns v .Allen, 2 B. M. , 246 .
3. Right ofjury toassessvalue or damages withoutproof.
The juryorjudge towhom the law and factsare submittedmay , in the absenceof
evidence,fixthevalueof work and laborfrom theirown knowledge (1 J.J. M. , 366; 3
Met., 127); and, from the descriptionof the propertyby a witness, the jurymay deter-
mine itsvalue(13 B. M. , 238),and may fixa highervalueupon itthan isfixedby him
(1 Mar.,68); and,inan actionforassaultand battery,upon a judgment by defaultand
in the absence of evidence, thejury may findmore than nominal damages . 2 Duv ., 419.
4. Verdictsin actionsagainstjointtrespassers.
According to the common law ,joint trespassersare jointlyand severallyliableto the
person injured, and he can maintain an action against all, or some , or one of them ; and
thisrulehasnotbeen changedby theCode. 4 Met .,333. Ifhe suemore thanone,thejury
should find a jointverdict against all, though they be guiltyin differentdegrees, each
and all being liablefor the entiredamages sustainedby the plaintiff. I Bibb, 440; 4
Id., 210 ; 4 J. J. M. , 268-69. A verdict against each for severaldamages is not void,
however ; but, as the plaintiffisentitledto but one satisfaction(5 Dana , 304),he can not
have severaljudgmentsupon suchverdict: and,notwithstandingthegeneralrulethatthe
judgment must conform to the verdict, in an action against two joint trespassers, if the
juryfindseveraland equaldamages againstthem, the plaintiffcan entera nolleprosequi
as to one, and takejudgment againstthe other forthe amount of the verdict against him ;
or he can takejudgment againstboth forsaidamount , upon enteringa remittituras to one
(Stone& Waters v .Matherly, 3 Mon. , 136); and , if thejury find unequal damages against
each, the plaintiffcan take judgment against both for the largest amount found against
either, without a remittituror nolleprosequi(4 Bibb, 210 ; 1 J. J. M. , 361 ; 4 Id., 268);
nor isthejudgment , iffor lessthan the damages laid in the declaration, affectedby the
factthatthe damages severallyassessedexceed that amount , that irregularitybeing cured
by thejudgment. Doughertyv.Dorsey, 4 Bibb, 210. And ,thejuryhavingfound a joint
verdictagainstfourdefendants,itwas heldthatthe courtactedproperlyin puttingthe
plaintiffupon terms that she should dismissher action against one, and (upon that being
done ) in renderingjudgment againstthe others for the amount of the verdict(Sheltonv.
Harlowe , 15 B. M. , 547); and , thejury having found a jointverdictagainstthree defend-
ants, itwas held that the court acted properlyin granting a new trialto one ,he being an
infantforwhom no guardian ad litemhad been appointed, and in allowing the judgment
to stand againstthe othersforthe amount of the verdict, without requiringa nolleprosequi
againstthe infant. Buckles v .Lambert, 4 Met ., 330.
But , in an actionagainstA , B ,C , and D , thejury having found a verdictagainstA and
B , and in favor of C and D ; A and B moved for a new trial, which was refused, and the
plaintiffmoved fora new trialas to C and D , which was also refused; and the Court of
Appealsheldthat,though themotionof A and B was admissible,yetthat, " theplaintiff
* " But if the trespass be not entire, and one defendant be guilty of part of it, and another be not
guiltyof that,but be guiltyof another part,then thejury not only may ,but should,findseveraldamages.
For itwould be unjustand illegalto render a judgment againsta party foran act of which he was shown

be several. But such casesoccur but seldom." 4. J. M. ,269.

§ 340 [369]. New trialdefined for what causesgranted. A new
trialis a re-examination in the same court of an issue of fact after a
verdictby a juryor a decisionby the court(a). The formerverdictor
decisionmay be vacatedand a new trialbe granted,on the application
ofthepartyaggrieved, forany ofthe followingcauses affectingmate-
riallyhissubstantialrights:
1.Irregularityin the proceedingsof the court, jury, or prevailing
party, or any order of court, or abuse of discretion, by which the
partywas preventedfrom havinga fairtrial(b).
2. Misconductof the jury, of the prevailingparty, or of his attor-
ney(c).
3. Accident or surprisewhich ordinaryprudence could not have
guardedagainst(d ).
4. Excessivedamages, appearingto have been given under the
influenceof passionor prejudice(e).
5. Error in the assessment of the amount of recovery, whether too
largeortoosmall,inan actionupon a contract(f ),orfortheinjury(g)
ordetentionof property(l/h).
6. That the verdict or decision is not sustained by sufficientevi-
dence(1), or iscontraryto law (j ).
7. Newly discoveredevidence, materialfor the party applying,
which he could not, with reasonablediligence, have discoveredand
produced at the trial(k).
8. Error of law occurringat the trialand excepted to by the party
makingtheapplication(1).
(a) GENERAL RULES AS TO NEW TRIALS .
1.Motion and grounds tobe of record.
The motionand groundsfora new trialcan onlybe filedby an orderof court,entered
on theorder-book,and theyshouldbe indorsedfiled. IMon. ,3; 78 Ky ., 536.
2. When a motionfor a new trialisnecessaryor allowable.
Aftera verdictby a jury,a motion for a new trialisnecessarytoenablea partyto
obtainfromtheCourtofAppealsa correctionoferrorsastoadmittingor excludingevi-
dence,orgivingor refusinginstructions(Humphrey v.Walton, 2 Bush, 580; Detheragev.
Montgomery,4 Id.,46); andsuchmotionisequallynecessarywhen,inanordinaryaction,
anissueoffacthas been decidedby thecourt, upon a submission,by waiverorotherwise,

overrulingUnionIns.Co.ofLouisvillev.Groom,4 Bush,289); and insuchcasesthe appel-
lantshouldrequirethecourtto " stateinwritingtheconclusionsof factfound separately
from the conclusionsof law ." 332.
But such motion isnot necessaryin an actionin equity(4 Bush,292),or upon the
trialof an attachment (84 Ky .,27); and an appeal may be taken,without such motion ,even
aftera trialby jury,but it placesonly mattersof recordbeforethe Court of Appeals.

But a motion to rehear a motion for a new trial, which has been overruled, is not
allowable; theonlyredressofthedefeatedlitigantisby appeal. LouisvilleRockand Lime
Co. v .Kerr, 78 Ky ., 12.
3. Effectofa motionfor a new trial.
Such motion,ifmade beforerenditionof judgment,suspendsproceedingson thever-
dict; as,theissuingof a writofinquiryastodamages (Wrightv.Haddock,7 Dana ,253);
and, ifmade afterjudgment, it suspendsproceedingsthereon; as, the issuingof an
executionor the prosecutionof an appeal(2 Dana ,335; LouisvilleChemicalWorks v.
Commonwealth,8 Bush, 179); and themotionmay be decidedat a subsequentterm; but
judgment isnot suspendedby a motion to setasidean orderoverrulinga motionfora
new trial(78Ky ., 14); and thecourtcan not prescribethe time forfilinga billof excep-
tionswhilstsuchmotionispending (10Bush,451-52); and itwas held thatthe deathof
a defendant,afterjudgment and whilsthis motion fora new trialwas pending,did not
ahate the action, though itwas upon a cause of actionwhich did not survive. Turner's
adm'r v .Booker, 2 Dana , 334.
A motion fora new trial,ifoverruled,doesnot preventa new motiontherefor,upona
ground not embraced in the firstmotion , ifa sufficientexcuse be made fornot so embracing
it(Hughesv.McGee , 1 Mar.,28); and a motionfora new trialdoesnotprevent,norisit
preventedby,a motion inarrestofjudgment. 7 Dana ,473.
4. Effectofgranting a new trial.
(1) A defendant,aftergettinga new trial,can notappealfrom the originaljudgment.
9 Bush , 69.
(2) If ajudgment inan actionatlaw ordecreeinchancerybe improperlysetaside,on
a motion fora new trialor a petitionfora rehearing, and a differentconclusionbe arrived
at in a secondjudgment or decree,the Court of Appealswillnot merelysetasidethe
latterbut willdirectthe former tobe re-instated. 2 Bibb, 177 ; 6 Mon. , 243 ; 6 Bush , 664.
5. Weight given toopinionofpresidingjudge.
" This courthas alwaysrequiredthatcauseshouldbe veryclearlymade out tojustify
the reversalof a judgment forrefusaltogranta new trial." King v.Walker, I Mar.,314-
But inEwing v.Price,3J.J. M. ,520, inwhich theonlygroundreliedon fora reversal
was thegrantingof a new trialto the appellee, the Court of Appeals,afterstatingfacts
which would havejustifiedthecourtbelow inrefusingthenew trial,said: " But thereis
a material and obvious distinctionin the grounds which will require the reversalofa
judgment overruling,and one granting,a new trial. By grantinga new trialthecaseisonly
kept open foranothertrialon themerits,and inthattrialeachpartyhasan equalchance
forjustice. But,by overrulinga motion for a new trial,the unsuccessfulpartyiscon-
cluded,and may be subjectedto irretrievablelossand irreparableinjustice. Therefore,
thiscourtwillbe more inclinedto sustaina judgment granting,than one overruling,a
new trial." And see Caldwell v .Wright,8 B. M. , 526 ; Ford v . Gregory'sheirs, 10 Id.,
182 ; Guthriev .Bogart, I Mar. ,334.

3. Impeachinga verdictby testimonyofjurors.
Jurorsare incompetent to impeach theirverdictby testifyingthat they had been tam-
peredwithby thesheriff(3 Mon.,415); or thata juror,notsworn asa witness,had stated
importantfactsto the other jurors(3 Mar. , 396), or thata juror, aftertheverdict, made

orreasonswhich influencedthejuryin theirfinding(Hardin, 586; 3 Mon., 59,60); or
thatone of thejurorsdid not agree to the verdict(Johnson v .Davenport, 3 J. J. M. , 390);
or,that an indictment, which was returned by competent jurors, was agreed to when
one of the jurorswas incompetent (Commonwealth v . Skeggs, 3 Bush , 19); or that the
verdictwas the resultof an agreement that such a verdict should be rendered as was
favoredby a majority of the jury (13 Bush , 651); or that, in an action for damages ,
a verdictwas arrived at by adding the sums which each juror was inclined to give
and dividingthe aggregate by twelve. Heath v . Conway , I Bibb, 398 ; Allardv . Smith,
2 Met ., 297 .
Jurorsare,however,competenttoprovethattheverdictrenderedwas not intended;
as,thatthejurorsagreed to findfor the plaintiffand to give him five shillingsdamages ,

tiffand the other for the defendant, and that theirforeman had , by mistake, delivereda
generalverdictforthe defendant(3 J. J. M. ,395); but the admissibilityof such testi-
mony " should be confined to casesofmistake clearlymade out,and which may be conceded

prietyof conduct." 3 J. J. M. ,395.

taken of Vance v .Haslett.

ielv.Holland,citedabove,istobe regardedassucha case. But inthatcase,asthecourt

ducing to show unfairness, before the chancellorwillrefuseto confirm thisact of his com-

§ 418 [449]. Lienon defendant'sproperty.-No lienon theproperty
of a defendant constructivelysummoned shallbe createdotherwisethan
by an attachmentas isprovidedin chapter3 oftitle8 or by judgment ;
nor shallany person be restrainedfrom paying or deliveringany money
orpropertyinhishands belongingor due to such defendant,by notice

In an action brought by A againsthis debtors, B , the defendant, was constructively
summoned,and a tractof land belongingto him was attached. B mortgaged the land,
pendentelite, to C ; who , having been made a defendant, filedan answer contestingthe
attachment, and prayingforan enforcementof the mortgage; and itwas held thatthe
attachmentwas voidforirregularities,and that,therefore,thecourthad no jurisdictionof
theactionor of the cross-petition. Petersv . Conway , 4 Bush , 571. In a note on page 39
of notesto my firsteditionof the Code I made these comments on that case:
" But, query; because, though the attachment was void, it seems that the court had
jurisdictionof theactionofA ; and might,on finalhearing,have ordereda saleof the
landforthe payment of A's demand , aftersatisfyingthe mortgage -debt. The court seems
tohave believedthat an attachment isnecessaryto give jurisdictionof an actionagainsta
defendant constructivelysummoned . This seems to be erroneous. An attachment isnot
an action; nor isa ground for an attachment a cause of action. Such ground need not be
statedin the petition; and , untilthe decisionwas rendered in Scottv .Doneghy , 17 В. М. ,
321, thereseemed to be good reason forbelievingthat such ground could be statedonly in
a separateaffidavit. 'This provisionalremedy is merely ancillaryand incidentalto the
actioninwhich itissought. It may be had eitherat or afterthe commencement of the
action. Moore v .Sheppard, 1 Met .,97. To make the jurisdictionof the court, as to an
action,depend upon thevalidityofan attachment obtainedin it,would reversethe rule,that
'theincidentfollowstheprincipal.' Section449 of the Code of 1854, of which 2418 of this
Code issubstantiallya copy,declared: No lienon thepropertyof a defendant constructively
summoned shallbe createdotherwise than by an attachment,as provided in chapter 3 of
title8,or byjudgment .' It seems clear that the legislaturecontemplated that an action
againsta defendant constructivelysummoned might proceed to judgment against him ;
and thatthejudgment might createa lienupon his property,though the plaintiffhad
failedtoattachit. Itseemsdifficultto perceiveupon what principleitcan be held that
an abortiveeffortto obtain an attachment-liencan affectthe jurisdictionof the court, or
therightsof the plaintiff,as totheaction."
I now concede, however ,thatthe court was rightin holding thatithad no jurisdiction;
becauseitisclearthat our courtscan not assume jurisdictionof an actionformoney
againstan absent defendant unlessit be shown that he has property subject to the juris-
dictionof thecourt(4 Dana ,146-47); and of coursethatfactcan notbe shown by a void
attachment; and I have learned, from examination of the record in the officeof the clerk
oftheCourtof Appeals,thatI erredinassumingthattheplaintiff'spetitionshowed that
thedefendanthad propertysubjecttothejurisdictionof the court; foritfailedtoallege

II. What isa forcibleentry.- A forcibleentry is-
1.An entrywithoutthe consent of the person having the actual
possession(a).

writof forcibleentryisconcerned,forceisimpliedfrom an entrywithoutconsenton land

7J.J.M. ,604; 14В. М. ,46); and,a fortiori,thiswritliesagainstonewho enterswithout
having a rightto the possession. 6 Bush , 493.
(b) When , and for what, a writ offorcibleentrylies.

erty,nor forthe shoresand bankswhich are used as necessaryincidentstothe ferry.
Litt. S. C. , 184 .
2.A personmay have a possessioninfactoflandwithoutresidingupon it,and though
itbe not cultivated, improved,or enclosed. 4 Bibb,389; 3 Mar.,347; I Litt.,225-26;
3 Id ., 397-98 .
And residenceupon partof a tract,claimingthewhole,thoughpartbe not inclosed,

And an entry upon uninclosedland with the intentto clear and fitit for cultivation

(a) 1.A surveyof unappropriatedlandgivestothe partyforwhom itismade a right

the weakness of his adversary's. Hendricks v. Bell,1 Bibb, 138 ; 2 Mar. , 31 ; 3 Id., 131.

" as in suitsin equity; " and itwas consequentlyheld that no billof exceptionswas

(e) It seems proper to callattentionto two distinctionsbetween former statutesand
the Code : 1. The act of 1796 (M. & B. ,279) providesthat,unlessa certifiedcopy of the
caveatbe lodged with the clerkof the proper court, within a prescribedtime," such caveat

In Sharpv.Curds,4 Bibb,547, itwas held thata caveatwas " determined" by the
plaintiff'sfailuretolodgea copy of itwiththeclerkwithintheprescribedtime; and that
thesubsequentissuingof a patentto him was not prohibitedby the statute. I am not
awareof any decisionastotheeffect,ifany,of theabovenamed changes.

(a) 1. Under chapter63 of the General Statutes, a married woman having a life-estate

that she can do so under 491 and 492 of the Code , if the remainder is contingent, or if
the reversionersor remainder-men are under disability(Gossom'v.McFerran , 79 Ky ., 236 ;
Munnell v .Orear,84 Id.,452): aliter, as to reversionsor vested remainders, the owners of
which are under no disability; for, as to the proceedings against them , 491 is uncon-
stitutional. Gossom v .McFerran ,supra.
2. According to the opinions in Henning v .Harrison, & c., 13 Bush ,723, and Munnell
v.Orear, & c., 84 Ky .,452, a court of equity " has no power ," upon the petitionof a mar-
riedwoman ,or upon thatof her and her husband ,to order a saleof land owned by her in
fee-simple, whether itbe her general or her separateestate; or to order a saleof her life-
estatein land ,whether itbe her general or her separate estate, except in connection with
a saleof the reversionor remainder as authorizedby ? 491. But I submit that the factthat
such an order would be erroneous does not prove that the court " has no power " to make
it; thegeneralrulebeingthat,when a courthasjurisdictionofthepersonand thesubject
of an action, itsorders, though erroneous,are not void.

vivorcan notjoininan actionagainstthe debtor;" and itwas held thatthe defendant's

action did ; and that a judgment in favor of the plaintiffswas erroneous (Morrison v .

(4 B. M. ,491 ; 13 Id.,332 ; 16 Id.,334 ; 2 Duv ., 521; 86 Ky .,135); failureto crediton the
judgment a payment admittedinthepetition(3 Met.,29; 1Bush,510; 4 Id.,353); failure,

182); " irregularitiesin the emanation and executionof the writof haberefaciaspossessionem”
(2 Bibb,238,); informalityinenteringjudgment on an award (2 Bibb,88); an order" that
courtadjournuntilcourt in course," the recordshowing thatthe court, on thenext

administratorto be levied de bonis propriis, in an action on a note executed by the
Entestate(3 J. J. M. , 299; 10 B. M. , 246); renderingjudgment against" the heirs"
of A without naming them , the record showing their names (83 Ky ., 309 ); rendering
judgment in favorof A and B ,on a noteallegedintheirpetitionto have been soldby
A to B ,and to belong to B (2 Met ., 302); rendering judgment in favor of A , he and

fendants(2 Duv .,4); and in an actionagainsttwo defendantswho pleadedpaymentof

infavorof hisco-plaintiff,B ,the defendant'spayment of the money toA , beforenotice

I. THE BURTHEN OF PROOF , GENERALLY .
I need not cite authoritiesto show that the burthen of proof lieson the party having
the affirmativeof an issue; as,when the plaintiffallegesthat the defendant executed the
writingsued on and thedefendantdeniesthathe did so,or when thedefendantalleges
thattheplaintiffhas releasedthe demand sued on and the plaintiffdeniesthathe has
done so. The onlydifficultygrows outof the question,who holdsthe affirmativeof the
issue or issues .
1. As toforming an issue.
(1) An issuemay be formed as well by the denialof a fact in the petitionand the
averment of it in the answer , as by the converse mode : thus, in an action for money ,
theplaintiff'saverment thatithas not been paid and the defendant'savermentthat it
hasbeenpaidform an issue,with theburthenof proofon the defendant. 3 B. M. ,376;
Vancev. Vance, 5 Mon. , 521.
(2) The question, whether a partyholdsthe affirmativeor the negativeof an issue,
dependson the substanceof the pleadingsand not on theuse of affirmativeor negative
languagein them . For example-

II . THE BURTHEN OF PROOF IN ACTIONS FOR MALICIOUS PROSECUTIONS .
1. As toactionsfor maliciousprosecutionsof criminalproceedings.
According to the opinion in Ullman v .Abrams ,9 Bush , 738, the nature of the defence
in which does not appear, the burthen lieson the plaintiffto prove both that the action
was maliciousand thatitwas without probable cause.
It isclearthatthe plaintiffmust allegeboth maliceand want of probablecause(see
note(2) ante,page 89),and theburthenof proofison him astoboth allegations, ifthey
are traversedby the defendant ; and such was the rule upon the plea of not guilty, under
the old practice. 4 J.J. M. , 629; 5 B. M. ,546. But thatplea isnot allowableunder
theCode (seenote(2)a,ante,page 121),and thedefendantmust traverseeachallegation;
orhe may ,even withouttraversingtheallegationof malice,filea specialpleaofprobable
cause (see4 J. J. M. ,628-29); for,asallthedecisionson thesubjecthold, theplaintiff
can not recover unless both malice and want of probable cause be shown ; and , conse-
quently,thequestionof maliceisimmaterial, ifprobablecausebe shown.
However ,not onlymay thejuryinfermalice(5 B. M. ,546),but thereisa primafacie
legalpresumptionof malice,from thewant of probablecause(Litt.S. C., 106; 7 Bush,
390); but the law does not implymalicefrom thefactthattheplaintiffwas acquittedby
theverdictof a jury,thoughitisconclusiveevidenceof hisinnocence. 4 J.J. M. ,630.
On theotherhand, thelaw impliesprobablecausefrom the factthattheplaintiffwas
indictedby a grand jury,unlessupon thetestimonyof the prosecutoralone(Garrardv.
Willett, 4J.J. M. ,628); and a judgment ina civilcase,againsta defendantwho appeared
and was heard,hasbeen held to be conclusiveevidenceof probablecause,though the
judgment may have been reversedby theCourtofAppeals(12 B. M. ,555; 18 Id.,846†);
and I perceiveno reasontodoubt thatthe same ruleappliestoa judgment of conviction
in a criminal case .
But want of probablecausecan not be inferredfrom proofof malicein a prosecution
forcrime (I B. M. ,359); for, " in thisrespect, even malicebecomes one of the safe-
guardsof society,and istheoccasionof bringingoffenderstojusticewho would otherwise
escape." Maddox v .McGinnis , 7 Mon. , 372.
As a plea of probable cause for a criminal prosecution, if sustained, constitutesa
defence, though the prosecutionmay have been malicious; and assuch a pleaimpliedly
admits malice, and , if unsustained, entitlesthe plaintiffto at least nominal damages,
the burthen of proofison the defendant(4 J. J. M. ,631; 3 Bush,83); though the
plaintiffhas the rightto prove factsshowing actualmalice(9 Bush,741), in orderto
enhance the damages intheeventof thedefendant'sfailuretosustainhisdefence. Gar-
rard v.Willett,4 J. J. M. , 628 ; Caskcy v .Lewis, 15 B. M. , 31 ; L. & N. R. Co. v . Brown,

by thisCode upon a ministerialofficer,and any act permittedto be
done by him , may be performed by hislawfuldeputy .

of thedeputy , with likewords or initials; and although chapter 110, 10, of the General
Statutesdeclares that a deputy -sheriff, in making return of a noticeor process, shallsub-
scribethe name ofhisprincipaland himself,ithas been heldtobe sufficientfora deputy
tosignthe name of his principalwithoutsigninghis own , or to sign hisown name as
deputy without signing his principal'sname (Norman v .Norman , & c., 6 Bush , 495 ; Tal-
bott'sheirsv.Hooser,12 Id.,408; Humphrey'sex'rv.Wade,84 Ky .,391); and an indorse-
ment on a writ, directed to the sheriffof Kenton County , by " W. F. Gillespie, D. S,"

uponthreeor more personsmay be exercisedby a majorityof them

§ 680 [751]. Affirmationinsteadofoath.- An oath requiredbythis
Code may be substitutedby the affirmationof a personwho iscon-
scientiouslyopposed to taking an oath.
§ 681 [752]. Mode ofcomputingdaysbetweenacts.-If a certainnum-
ber of days be required to intervene between two acts, the day ofone
only of the actsmay be counted .
" The ruleiswell settledthat where the computation isto be made from the actdone,
then theday on which itisdone must be included,but,iftobe made from thedayitself,
the day must be excluded." 80 Ky ., 306, and cases cited; 11 Bush , 220. For a lengthy
discussionof thissubjectsee Batman v .Megowan , I Met ., 533.
§ 682 [753]. Valid may be substitutedfordefectivebond. Ifa bond
providedforby thisCode be adjudged to be defective,a new andsuf-
ficientone may be executed in such reasonable time as the courtmay
fix, with thesame effectas iforiginallyexecuted.
If a party,desiringto appeal, attemptin good faithto executea bond, anddoes
execute,withsurety,such bond as ispreparedforhim by the clerk, and itbedefective,
he shouldbe allowedtoexecutea new bond, withoutprejudiceto hisrights; andrefusal
of permission to do so is ground for reversal. 2 Duv ., 76 ; 1 Bush , 223 ; 3 Id.,94; 79
Ky ., 259 : and see 7 Bush , 36, 37.
But ifno bond be given,bond can notbe given nunc pro tunc; and thesigningofa
blank paper, with authorityto the clerkto fillitup ,which he failedto do , was heldnot
tobe the givingof a bond (7 Bush,234); and an attachment-bond not executedbefore
the clerkisnot a defectivebond forwhich a new one can be substituted. Id., 131.
§ 683 [754]. Suretymay berequiredto make affidavitofqualifications.
An officerwhose duty itisto take a suretyinany bond providedfor
by thisCode may requirethepersonofferedas suretyto make affidavit
of his qualifications,which affidavitmay be made beforesuch officer.
The taking of such an affidavitshallnot exempt the officerfromany
liabilityto which he might otherwisebe subjectfortaking insufficient
security.
§ 684 [755]. Qualificationsofsurety.- The surety in every bond
providedfor by thisCode must be a residentof thisState,and be
worth double the sum to be secured beyond the amount of his debts,
and have propertyliableto executioninthisStateequalto thesumto
be secured . If there be two or more sureties in the same bond they
must , inthe aggregate,have the qualificationsprescribedin thissection.

RULES AS TO APPEALS FROM JUSTICES' COURTS , &C .
1.As towhatarefinaljudgments,from which onlyappealslie,seenoteI to?734.
2. As tothe" amount in controversy."
As thecasemust be " triedanew asifno judgment had been rendered," theamount in
controversyinthe courtappealedto,whethertheappealbe taken by the plaintiffor the
defendant,isnot necessarilytheamount ofthejudgment appealedfrom,but istheamount
whichwas incontroversyinthecourtappealedfrom. Millsv.Cauchman,4J.J.M. ,242;
Williamsv.Wilson,5 Dana ,596. Thus,inan actionina justice'scourtfor$90 damages,
theplaintiffrecoveredjudgment for$50,and on the defendant'sappealitwas heldthat
$90wastheamount in controversy. Donahue v.Murray, 2 Bush, 194.
And ,iftheplaintiffsuefor$50 orlessand thedefendantpleada set-offformore than
$50,theset-offistheamount incontroversy. Parkerv.Hulme ,3 Dana ,499.
3. As toassumptionofjurisdictionby thecourtappealedto.
AccordingtoElligev. Wilson, 1 J. J. M. , 588,ifan appealbe taken to the circuit
court,whichshouldhave been takentothe quarterlycourt,itwould be the duty ofthe
circuitcourttodismisstheappealand remittheappelleeto hisjudgment beforethejus-
tice; butitwas held inHughes v.Hardesty, 13Bush,364,thatthe circuitcourtmay try
thecaseunlessitsjurisdictionbe objectedto. The circuitcourtcan not,however,be
compelled,by agreementof parties,to trya casewhich ispending,upon appeal, inthe
quarterlycourt; theproceedingwas properlydismissed. Davisv.Davis,10 Bush,274.
4. Who may takean appeal,and itseffect.
(1) Eitherof severaldefendantsagainstwhom a judgment has been renderedmay
appeal,and theappealbringsbeforethecourtappealedtoallthosedefendants(Palmerv.

458 [TITE XVI.INFERIOR COURTS .
Kennedy ,& c., 7 J. J. M. ,498); but not persons who were not made defendants in the
court appealed from , eitherby summons or appearance. Claggetv .Blanchard,8 Dana ,42.
(2) A partyagainstwhom judgment isrenderedina justice'scourt,withoutsummons,
may appealand reverseitwithoutsubjectinghimselftoa judgment inthecourtappealed
to. Claggetv .Blanchard,8 Dana , 43 .
(3) When a cause isremoved by appeal from a justice'scourt to the circuitcourt,the
justicehas no furtherpower over it (Palmer v . Kennedy , 7 J. J. M. , 500 ; Shadrackv.
Milam ,6 Id.,608); unlesstheappealbe dismissed: seenextfollowingnote.
5. Dismissal ofappealor actionby thecourt.
The dismissalof an appeal reinstatesthe partieswhere they stood when itwas taken,
and deprivesthe appellatecourtofany power overthe case. Milesv.Reed,2J.J.M.,
137; Manier v. Lindsay, 3 Bush , 95 ; Olmsteadv . Mason , Id., 693 .
And such dismissalisproperiftheappellanthas failedto give an appeal-bondasis
requiredby law (Bledsoev. Cassady,2 Mar.,459; Manier v. Lindsay,3 Bush,95); or if
the appellant'swarrantwas properlydismissedby the courtappealedfrom, forwantof
jurisdiction(Fidlerv.Hall,2 Met.,461); or ifthe courtappealedto has no jurisdiction
of the appeal. Elligev. Wilson, 1 J. J. M. , 588; Milesv.Reed, 2 Id., 137; Jonesv.
Thompson's ex'r, 12 Bush , 394.
But , ifthe court appealed from had not jurisdictionof the subject of the action,and
the appellatecourthas jurisdictionof the appeal,itshouldnot dismissthe appealand
thus place the partieswhere they stoodbefore itwas taken ; but should dismissthe action
forwant ofjurisdictionin the courtappealedfrom,therebyvacatingitsjudgment and
remittingtheplaintifftohisproperremedy by a new action. McKitrickv.Peter,5 Dana,
587; Bassetv. Oldham,7 Id., 168; Howard v.Jones,2 B. M. , 526; Wiggintonv.Moss,2
Met.,38; Flemingv.Limebaugh,Id.,265; impliedlyoverrulingPollardv .Holeman,4 Bibb,
416, in which itwas held to be the duty of the circuitcourtto trythe case,de n.vo,
though thecourtappealedfrom had no jurisdiction.
6. Dismissalof appealby appellant.
A defendant who takes an appeal has no right to dismissitwithout the consentof the
plaintiff,who isentitledto have a trialon the merits(Semplev.Morrison,7 Mon.,298);
and itseemsnecessarilytofollowthata plaintiffwho takesan appealhas no righttodis-
miss itwithout the defendant'sconsent. And see ? 730 of Code .
7. Non -suitof theplaintiff.
In Shadrackv.Milam,6 J. J. M. ,608, itwas held that,upon an appealby thede-
fendant,the appellee,who stoodin the appellatecourtas plaintiffin the cause,had a
right, " beforethejuryhad retiredfrom the bar toconsiderof theirverdict" (seeCode,
371),totakea non-suit; but thatno executioncouldbe issuedon thejudgmentappealed
from .
8. Irregularitiesinproceedingsof,orbefore,thecourtappealedfrom.
An act of 1812(M. & B.,894),declaredthat" no appealshallbe dismissedforany
irregularityintheproceedingshad beforethemagistrate; but thesame shallbe triedupon

II . WHO MAY APPEAL .*
Section734 declares that an appeal shall be granted " to a party or privy against a
party or privy."
1. As toparties.
The following have been held tobe partieswho can appeal: One through whose land a
roadhas been opened,by the countycourt,though he had not been summoned as he
should have been (I Bibb, 294); one to whom the plaintiffassignsthe claim sued on or
landsued for,pendentelite(7J.J. M. ,302,642); one whose land was soldwhilsthe was
an.infantupon the ex partepetitionof his guardian(9 Dana , 526); a guardianad litem
forinfants,though theirstatutoryguardianmay have acceptedand receiptedfor the
money towhich theywere entitledunderthejudgment appealedfrom (8 Bush,69); a gar-
nishee, thoughnot made a defendant,againstwhom an attachingcreditorhas obtaineda
judgment (17 B. M. , 631; Smith v.Gower, 3 Met., 171); a creditorwho has proved a
claim in an actionbrought under the act of 1856 (Heidrich& Co. v.Silva, & c., II
Ky . L. R.,645); a bidderfor propertyat a decretalsalewhose bid is rejectedby the
court(1 Bush,12; 9 Id.,285; Hughesv.Swope, 11 Ky .L. R., 783), or who iscompelled
to complywith his bid, notwithstandinghis exceptions(3 Bush, 491; Pittman,& c.,v.
Wakefield,& c.,11 Ky . L. R.,972); and a city,though theactionwas againstitsmayor
and council. 18 В. М. , 13.
And an appealfrom an order appointinga committeefora personof unsound mind
must be prosecutedin his name . 5 Dana , 59 .
And though, in general, a partywhose proceedingshave been so erroneousthata
judgment in hisfavorwould have been reversedcan not obtaina reversalof a judg-
ment againsthim , though erroneous (3 Bibb, 225 ; 6 J. J. M. , 480); ithas been held that,
notwithstandingsuch errorsof the plaintiff, iftherehas been a trialon the meritsresulting,

And a defendantcan appealfrom a judgment which he has repleviedor paid. Keller
v .Williams , 10 Bush , 221 .
And , if the defendant separatea demand which would otherwisehave been indivisible,
by contestingonlya partof it,the plaintiff'scoercionor acceptanceof satisfactionofa
judgment for the uncontested part does not bar his right to appeal from a judgment
againsthim astothecontestedpart. 13 Bush,407; 80 Ky.,576and 585.
And a plaintiffobtaining judgment for part of a contested, indivisibledemand , can
appealfrom thejudgment though inhisfavor(6 Mon., 115; Wade v.FirstNationalBank
of Franklin, 11 Bush , 699); but itwas settledthat a plaintiff, afterreceivingsatisfaction
of, or whilst insistingon , such a judgment , could not maintain an appeal in order to
recovermore : " such an actionisan entirety, and a trialdisposesof the whole case, and a
new trialmust be likewise of the entire matter in issue." Wade v . First National Bank
of Franklin, supra ; Payne v .Woolley, 80 Ky ., 568 ; Brown v.Vancleave, 86 Id., 381 ; and
see Allen v.Feland, 10 B. M. , 306. See, however , act of March 24, 1888, amending
2757 of Code , and comment thereon.
And itwas heldthata defendant,a part of whose propertyhad been attached,and
who had assigned allhis propertypendente lite, could maintain an appeal from an order
sustainingthe attachment; because, if he should obtain a reversal, he would have a right
to recoverdamages fora maliciousprosecutionof the attachment- a rightwhich was not
assignable. Francis v . Burnett, 84 Ky ., 23. Query, fora reason stated in note 6 , ante,
page 225 .
And though theCode (2745) authorizesan infantto appealwithin oneyearafterhe
becomes of age,he may do sobeforehe becomes of age. 79 Ky .,40.
BUT THE APPELLANT MUST HAVE A DIRECT AND SUBSTANTIAL INTEREST IN OB-
TAINING A REVERSAL ; for, conforming to the former law , the Code (2756 ) declaresthat
no judgment shallbe reversedor modified" exceptforan errortothe substantialrights
of the party complaining thereof."
Thus , a merchant or innkeeper or warehouse-man can not maintain an appeal from an
order of the county court refusingto establisha public road through the lands of others,
though he might be incidentallybenefitedthereby (3 Bibb, 79) ; nor can the Common-
wealth do so(5 Mon. , 23 ); nor can a person maintain an appeal from an order refusingto
abate a publicnuisanceunless he has sustaineda specialinjuryfrom it. I Bibb, 293; 10
Bush ,292 .
And the owners of land in a proposed pond -draining districtcan not maintain an
appeal from an order establishingthe boundary of such district,no tax having been levied
by thepond-drainingcompany,aswas authorizedby itscharter. Combsv.JeffersonPond-
draining Co., 3 Met .,72 : see CypressPond -draining Co. v .Hooper,etal., 2 Met ., 350.
Nor willthe court reverse a judgment against the plaintiffin a triflingor frivolous
action,or to enablea partyto recover,or to relievehim from, a trivialsum . 2 Bibb,
542-43 ; 3 Id., 474; 5 J. J. M. , 531 ; 1 Dana , 357 ; 83 Ky ., 245 ; Luckett v . Clark &

inEngland is considered as triflingin this respectwhen the sum to be recovered isless
than £ 20. 2 Tidd's Prac. 819. Without deciding what sum should be recoverableto

III . NECESSARY PARTIES TO APPEALS .
1. As toappellants.
In Wellsv.Wells,4 Mon., 152,itwas heldthateachpartyina willcasehad a rightto
maintain a separatewrit of error, and that, " although more than one may join, yet the
writ is to be treated as a several writ ."
But,ingeneral,allpartiesagainstwhom a jointjudgment isrenderedmustjoininan
appeal to reverse it (2 Bibb, 392 ; 1 Mar. , 22 ; 1 Dana , 366 ; 1 B. M. , 69) ; unlesssome
refusetojoin,inwhich event,underthe old practice,the partydesiringa reversalprose-
cuted a writ of error, and " then,by summons and severance,he might proceed on hisown
casealone,and exempt himself from any eventual liabilityfor the others." 2 Bibb, 392;
2 J.J. M. , 72. Why theproceedingof summons and severancewas not allowedupon
appeals, I have not seen stated, but itseems clear that itwas not allowed because appeals
were granted only by the court renderingthe judgment . It seems clear, at any rate, that
theCode has substitutedappealsgrantedby the clerkof theCourt ofAppealsforwrits
of error, and that a party wishing to have a summons and severance should obtainan ap-
peal from the clerk.
But severaldefendantscan notjoin in an appealfrom a judgment directingeachof
them to convey a separatetractof land,or to pay a separatesum of money , or to do other
separateacts(1 Mar., 22; 4 Dana , 179-80; 82 Ky .,473); unlesstherebe a jointjudg-
ment againstthem forcosts. 7 Mon., 591; 2J.J. M. ,72.
2. As to appellees.

(2) Upon a writof errortoreversean orderof the countycourtbindingthe plaintiff
inerroras an apprentice, the personto whom he was bound was the properand only

(3) On a writ of errorto reversea decree under which land was sold to satisfya mort-
gage, thecourtaffirmedthejudgment,but refusedtodecideon thevalidityof thesale,as
the purchaser was not a party to the proceeding. 6 J. J. M. , 335-36 .
(4) In an actionby A's creditoragainsthim and hiswife to subjectland allegedto
have been fraudulentlyconveyedto her,subjecttoa mortgage to K , who was made a
party, thecourtordereda saleof thelandtopay theplaintiff'sdemand and themortgage-
debt; and theappealof A and wifewas dismissedbecauseK was notmade a partytothe
appeal, the courtsaying: " Whoever isa partyto the recordin the courtbelow,and

But,undertheoldpractice,an appeal,which couldbe grantedonlyby thecourtwhich

by theclerkof the Court of Appeals,theappellantmust statethe names of theappel-
lees,as they must be summoned or warned to appear (27361.

of the appellees, unless their names are unknown , whether the appeal be grantedby the

IV . AMENDMENTS OF APPEALS AS TO PARTIES .
" According to the settledpracticeof this court, a writ of errorincludingthe names
of too many may be amended by strikingout the names of defendantswhich areinserted
improperly, leaving the writ valid as to thosewhose names are properly there, although
names can not be insertedwhich were at firstimproperly omitted." Chambers v .Wilkins,
2 Litt., 145. But in Carr v.Callaghan, 3 Litt.,377, which , aswell as Chambers v .Wilkins,
was a case in equity,the courtsaid: " Itisan establishedpractice,thatpartieswho are
not partiesto the writ of error, but who are or may be affectedby a reversal, shall be
summoned and thus made partieshere, before the cause is tried. This we have directed
in the present instance; but have receivedfor answer ,that the administratrixof Pauling
isdead , and that there isnow no personal representative. Under such circumstances, we
conceive that we can not protractthe cause, now too ancient in every court where ithas
been, any longer,and thatwe can not refuse,forthiscause,theplaintiffsinthewritof
errorthatrelieftowhich theyare entitled. But we can notgive any decree,ordirectit
tobe done in the courtbelow , operatingagainstPauling'sestate."
An act of 1827 (M. & B., 137) declared " that it shall be lawful for the Court of
Appeals to amend writsof error, by eitherpermittingadditionalpartiesto be made , when
necessary, or, where unnecessary partiesare made , to strikeout such unnecessaryparty;
and tomake any othernecessaryamendments toenablethem totrythecause,any custom
or usage to the contrarynotwithstanding."
That act seems to me to be now in forceas to appeals granted by the clerkof the Court
of Appeals , though not as to appealsgranted by the court below . See foot-note,ante,
page 465 .
For decisionsupon that act, see Castleman v .Holmes, 7 Mon. , 592 ; McKee's heirsv .
Hann and McKee , 9 Dana , 526.

(e) As towhat constitutesajudgment .
Upon thissubjectthere has been some conflictof opinion. In Bruce v . Fox , I Dana ,
449, the courtsaid: " Discretionaryacts(such, for example, as an appointmentof a
clerkor the removal of a guardian ) which do not establishor violateany right, defined
and guarranteed by law , are not judicial, and are, therefore, not directlyrevisableby this
court ."
Thus , in Piatt v . Alloway, 2 Bibb, 554, itwas held that a writ of error did not lieto
an orderofthe countycourtremovinga guardian,and the courtsaid: " The removalof
an officer, the tenure of whose officeis the will of a superior, isin allgovernments con-

sideredan executivefunction. A guardianbeinga privateand not a publicofficerdoes
notchangethecharacterofthepower exertedinhisremoval. Whether theofficebe public
or private,thenatureand characterof the power ofremovalwillbe thesame." And in
Taylorv.Commonwealth,3 J. J. M. ,401,itwas heldthata writof errorwould notlieto
an orderofthecountycourtdeclaringthatA had vacatedtheofficeof clerkofsaidcourt
and appointingB to fillthe supposedvacancy,as theactionof the courtwas executive
and not judicial. But in Bruce v. Fox,citedsupra, a majorityof the courtheld that
ordersof the circuitcourtrefusingto qualifyB and qualifyingF, as Commonwealth's
attorney,were judicialorders. And inIsaacsv.Taylor,3 Dana ,600,ordersofthecounty
courtremovingA and appointingB , asguardian,were held to be judicialorders. And
thatdecisionwas citedwith approval in Gorham v . Luckett, 6 B. M. , 146, in which itwas
heldthat,under a statuteauthorizingcountycourtstoremove jailers" wheneveritshall
appear tothem thatsuch jailershave been guiltyofneglectof duty," an orderremoving
a jailerwas a judicialorder. And , under a statuteauthorizingeach countycourttosus-
pend thejailerofthecounty" from actingas such,forsuch periodasitmay deem right,
when thepublicinterestshallimperativelyrequirethesame," an ordersuspendingajailer
was held to be a judicialorder. Lowe v. Commonwealth,3 Met , 237; and seeSmithv.
Cochran,& c.,7 Bush,147. And itissettledthatan appointmentor removalofan admin-
istratorisa judicialorder. 6 B. M. , 146, and cases cited. And an order disbarringan
attorney,even by a courtactingmerelyex officio, isa judicialorder. IDana ,450,argu-
endo ; Rice v . Commonwealth , 18 B. Μ .,472 .
In Taylorv.Commonwealth, citedsupra,the courtsaid: "Although the Constitution
has confidedto the courtsthe appointmentof theirown clerks, stillthe natureofthe
power isnot changed. It isessentiallyexecutive,whensoeveror by whomsoever itmay
be exercised. It isas much executivewhen exercisedby a courtas by the governor."
But inGorham v.Luckett,citedsupra,thecourtsaid: " The law definesand regulatesthe
rightsand interestsof individuals,onlyby declaringor determiningthe factson which
thoserightsand interestsare to depend, and by which they are to be decided. Any
legitimateact which directlyaffectssuch rightsand interests, and establishesor destroys
them ,necessarilyimpliesa decisionupon the factsand the law upon which thoserights
aremade to depend; and, therefore, everysuch act isin itselfessentiallyjudicial,by
whatever tribunalitmay be exercised. If the power of doing suchan act be vestedin
the executive department of the government , the act itselfisnot the lessjudicialinits
natureor essence, but the power under which itisdone being, in the distributionof
power,vestedintheexecutiveand not in thejudiciary, itis,inthe constitutionalsense,
an executiveand nota judicialpower, and,beingentrustedsolelyto thejudgmentofthe
executive, is withdrawn from the controlof the courts. But the same power, when
vested in a court, is not only in essence but in form , and in the constitutionalsense,a
judicialpower. If vested in a tribunal of a mixed character , like the county
court, itisto be referredto itscharacteras a judicialbody, becauseitisin itsnature
and effecta judicialpower. And , ifthe questionwere doubtful, the power itselfor
an actdone under itought to be regardedas judicial,ratherthan to closeagainstthe
partyaggrievedby itsabusethe common remedy by appealorwritof error." 6 B. M.,
160, 164.
It has been saidthatno appealliesfrom a decisionupon a writof habeascorpus,

make outofcourt." 15 B. M. , 153. But undera statutewhich authorizedthe condem-
nationof landfora turnpikecompany by proceedingsbeforea judge out ofcourt,itwas
held thathisactswere judicialand subjectto revisionby the Court ofAppeals. Rica,
& c.,v .Danville, Lancaster& NicholasvilleTurnpike Company , 7 Dana , 81 .
